data.title: Bond yields tumble as weak jobs report raises chances of a half-point Fed rate cut
data.description:
By Joy Wiltermuth

Longer-term Treasury yields look like a safety play for the first time since April's market shock

U.S. Treasurys look to be back in the flight-to-safety camp.

U.S. Treasury yields fell dramatically on Friday, as traders braced for the Federal Reserve to potentially cut interest rates more deeply than previously anticipated to shore up a dimming jobs market.

Friday's August jobs report reflected a four-month stalling streak in the labor market, including a surprising revision to June data that indicated the economy lost jobs that month.

"I don't think people were expecting to see that negative month," said George Catrambone, head of fixed income, Americas, at DWS - noting that the latest revisions to many job categories outside of healthcare showed negative growth for the first half of this year. "We haven't necessarily been pricing in that."

As a result, 2-year Treasury yields BX:TMUBMUSD02Y tumbled by about 12 basis points to 3.47%, at last check, while the all-important 10-year yield BX:TMUBMUSD10Y was 9 basis points lower at 4.07%, according to FactSet data.

"The real question now becomes how many rate cuts follow, and from our lens the persistent weakening in the labor market supports our ongoing call for a total of 75 basis points of rate reduction by year-end," Kathy Bostjancic, chief economist at Nationwide, said in emailed comments.

The odds of the Fed greenlighting a bigger rate cut of 50 basis points in September were close to 14%, according to the CME FedWatch Tool, but still 86% for a regular cut of 25 basis points.

Steve Englander, Standard Chartered Bank's global FX strategist, earlier this week laid out a case for why a jumbo rate cut this month may be justified, arguing that markets and policymakers have been misreading labor data.

To be sure, next week's inflation data could shake up the narrative again, but for now it looks like stagflation risks - or elevated inflation combined with a wobbling economy - aren't a primary concern for investors.

With the labor market "flatlining," risks that could create a potential recession were back in focus, said Jeffrey Schulze, head of economic and market strategy at ClearBridge Investments. That's changed the tone from the initial morning reaction to drag on stocks, he noted, with investors starting to price in slower growth and a less robust earnings backdrop than was initially anticipated.

Treasury debt back in style

That backdrop, with the Fed potentially needing to protect the economy with rate cuts, was putting a fresh shine on the $29 trillion Treasury market.

Investors for much of this year tended to hit the sell button in tandem on U.S. stocks and Treasurys, particularly in longer-duration 10-year notes and 30-year bonds BX:TMUBMUSD30Y during pockets of turmoil. That's raised concerns that U.S. assets could be losing their luster.

Investors instead have been favoring gold (GC00) and other traditional flight-to-safety assets except for the dollar DXY, which was lower Friday and down 10% this year.

Yet with fiscal concerns driving global government bond yields higher across developed markets, U.S. Treasurys may once again look like "one of the cleanest of some dirty shirts in the laundry pile," said Catrambone at DWS.

The fresh appeal of U.S. Treasurys on Friday had the 2-year yield on pace for a new low this year, while the 10-year rate was moving closer to its sub-4% low set in the wake of President Trump's April tariff shock.

That could reflect a move by investors to lock in yields while they still can. There's still an estimated $7.3 trillion parked in U.S. money-market funds, a shorter-term cash surrogate. Those investors could face reinvestment risks down the road if yields rapidly dwindle as the Fed cuts rates.

Earlier this week, a jump in bond yields helped pressure stocks lower. The S&P 500 SPX and the Nasdaq Composite COMP on Friday briefly roared to fresh intraday records - but all three major U.S. stock indexes, including the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA, have since turned negative.

Schulze at ClearBridge said he think this is just a soft patch for stocks and "a buy-the-dip" opportunity. He thinks stocks can move higher in 2026 as stimulus from Trump's signature tax and spending bill kicks in, as the Fed cuts rates and as there's more clarity on the tariff front.

See: U.S. unemployment rate jumps to nearly 4-year high. Weak 22,000 increase in August jobs report tees up Fed rate cut.

-Joy Wiltermuth

data.title: Black Unemployment Rises Again in Jobs Report — WSJ
data.description:
By Konrad Putzier

The racial gap in the labor market is widening. The unemployment rate for Black Americans rose to 7.5% in August, the Bureau of Labor Statistics said Friday. That's up from a seasonally adjusted 7.2% in July and 6.0% in May.

Meanwhile, the white unemployment remained unchanged at a seasonally adjusted 3.7% in August and was slightly lower than in May.

Economists say that rising unemployment among Black Americans is a potential warning sign for the broader labor market. Black workers are more likely to hold low-skilled or entry-level jobs, which are often cut first when the economy weakens. The share of Black Americans who are unemployed is at the highest level since October 2021.

The Hispanic or Latino unemployment rate rose to 5.3% in August from 5.0% in July, although it declined slightly on a year-over-year basis.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Brownmine Launches Appli+AI to Democratize AI Technology for Small and Midsize Businesses
data.description:
Atlanta, Georgia--(Newsfile Corp. - September 5, 2025) - Brownmine officially launched Appli+AI on September 2, 2025, introducing a comprehensive AI automation, consulting, and education service specifically designed for small and midsize businesses (SMBs) and underconnected communities. The new platform addresses a critical market gap where advanced AI technology has remained largely inaccessible to smaller organizations with limited resources.

Appli+AI delivers four core value propositions to bridge the technological divide. The service focuses on productivity enhancement through task automation, scalable systems that grow with businesses, measurable ROI through efficiency and revenue increases, and practical education that demystifies AI technology for everyday business applications.

Segment-Specific Solutions for Diverse Business Needs

The platform offers tailored solutions across multiple business categories to maximize practical impact. Local businesses can implement AI agents for 24/7 customer service, potentially boosting service quality without additional staff costs. Marketing automation and inventory management systems streamline operations and may improve customer engagement

Nonprofits and community leaders gain access to AI-powered data analysis for potentially better resource allocation and impact measurement. Grant writing assistance and volunteer management tools aim to help maximize organizational effectiveness.

Entrepreneurs potentially benefit from streamlined marketing campaigns, automated lead generation, and AI-powered business planning tools. These solutions reduce operational overhead while aiming to accelerate growth potential.

Professional service providers across industries can leverage AI for everyday productivity enhancement, including automated scheduling, proposal drafting, and client communication management. These applications deliver immediate value while requiring minimal technical expertise from users.

Challenging Traditional AI Implementation Models

The launch represents a significant departure from the current AI implementation landscape where technical expertise and substantial financial resources have been prerequisites for adoption. Appli+AI positions advanced AI technology as an equalizer that enables smaller organizations to compete effectively with larger enterprises.

Traditional AI deployment has favored corporations with dedicated IT departments and substantial budgets. Appli+AI eliminates these barriers by providing turnkey solutions that require no specialized technical knowledge or significant upfront investment from users.

"Every business, every entrepreneur, and every community deserves the chance to innovate, scale, and thrive with AI," said Mike A. Williams, Founder and CEO of Brownmine. "AI should not be reserved for big corporations. Appli+AI exists to make sure no one is left behind in this technological revolution."

Broader Economic Impact and Innovation Acceleration

Beyond immediate business applications, the Appli+AI launch signals a fundamental shift in how AI technology may be distributed across the economic spectrum. By making advanced tools accessible to previously underserved segments, the initiative could accelerate innovation in sectors that have been left behind in the AI revolution.

The service creates new models for technology democratization that prioritize practical application over theoretical concepts. This approach focuses on delivering tangible business value through automation tools that integrate seamlessly into existing workflows and operations.

Small businesses and community organizations can now access the same caliber of AI technology that has previously driven competitive advantages for major corporations. This technological leveling represents a potential catalyst for widespread economic transformation across multiple sectors.

About Brownmine and Appli+AI

Brownmine developed Appli+AI as a comprehensive solution for organizations seeking to harness AI technology without the traditional barriers of technical complexity and resource requirements. The service combines automation tools, strategic consulting, and practical education to deliver measurable business outcomes.

SMBs, entrepreneurs, and community leaders can explore resources, training, and tailored AI solutions through our websites. The service emphasizes accessibility and practical application while maintaining the sophisticated capabilities typically associated with enterprise-level AI implementations.

For more information about Appli+AI and its tech solutions for small and midsize businesses, visit www.appliai.ai or ww.brownmine.com to contact our team for additional details and demonstration opportunities.

Media Contact:

Charlene Allen

Operations and Client Manager

Email: charlene@brownmine.com

To view the source version of this press release, please visit https://www.newsfilecorp.com/release/264951

data.title: Toronto Stocks Inch Higher, Enghouse Systems Fall on 3Q Profit, Revenue Declines
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto-listed stocks edged forward.

Sector performance was mixed Friday. Primary losses in energy, tech services, and commercial services offset gains in materials, tech and health services stocks.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index rose by 0.2% to 28972.10 and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 was virtually unchanged at 1715.55.

On the employment front, Canada's unemployment rate rose more than expected in August, hitting 7.1%, its highest since May 2016 outside of the height of the pandemic. According to Statistics Canada, the economy lost a net 65,500 jobs last month, on top of 40,800 lost the month before. Market expectations were for employment to rise by 10,000.

Shares in Enghouse Systems fell by 8.8% to 20.82 Canadian dollars ($15.06) after reporting lower profit and revenue in the fiscal third quarter as the technology company grapples with macroeconomic uncertainties.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Tesla’s Musk pay scheme mixes two kinds of madness
data.description:
The carmaker’s board wants to gift $1 trln to its boss with a new incentive plan. He has to hit goals that start from the weak benchmarks of past promises but rise to aptly far-fetched targets, like $8.5 trln in market value. The trade-off: speeding Musk’s path to control.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Jonathan Guilford on X and Linkedin.

CONTEXT NEWS

Electric-car maker Tesla disclosed in a securities filing on September 5 a new proposed incentive award scheme for Chief Executive Elon Musk. Under the plan, Musk would receive up to 12 tranches of restricted stock awards, up to nearly 424 million at most, representing a 12% stake.

To unlock each tranche, Tesla must hit one market capitalization milestone and one operational milestone. Awards begin at a market value of $2 trillion with the final tranche achieved if the market value reaches at least $8.5 trillion by 2035.

data.title: Arabica Futures Slip as Tariff Uncertainty Loom
data.description:
Arabica coffee futures fell toward $3.85 per pound on Friday as traders awaited clarity on whether US President Donald Trump’s proposed 50% tariff on imports from top supplier Brazil would be enforced.

Prices have been volatile, climbing to three-month highs in August before easing in early September.

The market remains underpinned by supply concerns, speculative buying, and dwindling certified stocks, with the tariff threat adding further pressure.

Brazil’s 2025/26 Arabica crop is forecast at 35.2 million bags, down 11.2% from the previous harvest, though overall production is expected to edge higher on improved productivity.

Weather risks—including drought, irregular rainfall, and frost—are adding to uncertainty, while global inventories sit at historically low levels.

With limited certified stocks and constrained availability from alternative origins, short-term supply challenges continue to support prices.

data.title: Apple's $9B Power Move in India Changes Everything
data.description:
Apple just quietly posted its strongest year yet in India, with annual revenue climbing to nearly $9 billion through Marchup 13% from the $8 billion it made the year before. That growth, while still a blip in Apple's global pie, signals something bigger: India is no longer a slow-burn optionality story. It's turning into a real engine. iPhones were the key driver once again, helped by credit-card partnerships, trade-in deals, and a barrage of student discounts designed to offset India's steep import taxes. According to a person familiar with the numbers, demand for MacBooks also rose sharply, hinting at a broader upshift in Apple's brand equity across the country's rising middle class.

The company's moves on the ground back up the numbers. In just the past week, Apple added two new flagship stores in Bangalore and Pune, with Noida and Mumbai next on deck. That retail rollout follows its 2023 decision to carve India into a standalone sales regiona structural move that reflects how Apple's senior team now sees the market. As of now, iPhones command about 7% market share locally, but management appears to be playing the long game. Apple devices in India carry higher price tags than in the USan entry-level iPhone 16 costs nearly $907 there versus $799 statesidebut CEO Tim Cook has repeatedly called India one of Apple's fastest-growing geographies.

Behind the scenes, India is also becoming a second factory floor. One out of every five iPhones is now made in the country, and Apple is actively scaling up capacity across five plants, including two that just came online. That shift has been years in the makingbut with Chinese production facing renewed scrutiny, it's now more relevant than ever. Cook reportedly plans to use India as a key node in the company's US-bound supply chain, in part to reduce dependence on China. Taken together, Apple's retail expansion, surging sales, and supply chain localization suggest that India may be stepping into the role China once played: not just a growth market, but a strategic hedge.

HelloFresh said co-founder Thomas Griesel would leave the company by the end of April.

data.title: US NY FED STAFF NOWCAST ESTIMATES US 3Q GDP GROWTH RATE AT 2.1%, Q4 AT 2.3%, BOTH DOWN 0.1 PERCENTAGE POINT FROM PREVIOUS, AFTER LATEST WEAK JOB DATA
The Jobs Report Was Bad. It's Hitting Bank and Brokerage Stocks. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andrew Welsch

Shares of Charles Schwab, Robinhood, other brokerage firms, and banks dropped after a disappointing jobs report raised concerns the economy is weakening and increased the likelihood the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates.

On Friday, the Bureau of Labor Statistics said the U.S. economy added just 22,000 jobs in August, well below the 76,500 analysts were expecting. The government also revised nonfarm payrolls for June and July lower. If the Federal Reserve lowers interest rates to spur economic growth, that likely would hurt net interest income, a key source of profit for banks, brokerages, and wealth management companies. NII is the difference between what banks earn on interest-bearing assets and the interest they pay to depositors.

"Softer economic data this morning, broader equity market pressure, and the prospect of falling interest rates are all inputs that could impact brokerage stocks. Interest-rate implications for brokerage firms are nuanced, with puts and takes, but lower rates could create some headwinds," said Devin Ryan, equity research analyst at Citizens. Financial stocks were dropping Friday. The Vanguard Financials ETF, which tracks a basket of financial companies, was down 1.7%, versus a 0.5% decline for the S&P 500 index. The KBW Bank Index was down 1.8%. Schwab's stock was down 5.5%. Robinhood and Interactive Brokers shares were down 3.9% and 5.9%, respectively. Morgan Stanley and Stifel Financial, both which have wealth management and investment bank units, dropped 1.7% and 2.5%, respectively.

The nation's largest consumer banks also took hits: Wells Fargo's stock declined 3.0%. Shares of Bank of America and JPMorgan Chase fell 1.7% and 2.6%, respectively.

Friday's jobs report adds to recent economic data pointing to a jobs market and economy under pressure from tariffs and trade policy uncertainty. Sluggish economic growth, or even a recession, could hurt banks by dampening demand for loans and spurring more defaults. Brokerage and wealth management clients may also be less inclined to put more money in their investing accounts. Shareholders have been keeping close tabs on the ability of Schwab and other companies to bring in net new assets.

Investors' Friday jitters contrast with recent bullish sentiment and strong second-quarter earnings at banks and brokerage firms, which were lifted by a trading boom as brokerage customers borrowed from their firms to buy stocks. Margin debt topped $1 trillion in June, a record, according to data from Finra, the brokerage industry's self-regulatory organization. Shares of Schwab and Interactive Brokers are up this year 24% and 37%, respectively. Robinhood's stock is up 157% even after a sharp selloff in stocks in April when President Donald Trump unveiled sweeping tariffs.

Whether investors will ultimately brush off today's bad news as a temporary setback remains to be seen.

Write to Andrew Welsch at andrew.welsch@barrons.com

data.title: Mexican Peso Rebounds on USD Weakness
data.description:
The Mexican peso strengthened past 18.7 per USD as the greenback weakened sharply, easing pressure on the currency despite lingering domestic growth concerns.

Weaker US payrolls drove a drop in short-term Treasury yields, eroding the dollar’s appeal.

Domestically, Banxico’s upgraded growth projections, lifting its 2025 GDP forecast to 0.6% and 2026 to 1.1%, offered some support by signaling resilience in the economy.

Still, headwinds persist, with remittances down 4.7% in July from a year earlier, marking a fourth straight monthly decline and removing a key source of support for the peso.

Unemployment also edged up to 2.8% from 2.7%, hinting at softer consumption and reinforcing the case for further easing.

Banxico has begun a measured cutting cycle, lowering rates by 25bps to 7.75% and signaling a cautious, data-dependent path that diminishes the peso’s carry trade appeal.

data.title: MTN not in talks to acquire Telkom, CEO says
data.description:
Africa's biggest mobile operator MTN Group is not in active talks about reviving a potential acquisition of smaller South African rival Telkom , either with Telkom or internally, group CEO Ralph Mupita said on Friday.

Mupita made the comments after Bloomberg reported on Thursday, citing people familiar with the matter, that MTN is considering reviving talks to acquire Telkom, which has the country's largest fibre assets.

"There are no talks with advisers between ourselves and Telkom. We did discuss this at some time in the past, but there are no active discussions," Mupita told reporters. He added that he could not rule out future talks.

Telkom's shares jumped 13% on Thursday on the news. On Friday, shares closed 8% weaker.

In 2022, MTN terminated early-stage buyout talks with Telkom after failing to reach an agreement.

Mupita has emphasized that in South Africa, as in Europe, the market needs to consolidate, both at the levels of infrastructure and telecom operator, to about two mobile network operators from the current four.

He said profit pools are limited in South Africa, "and we see that in the margins that are being generated, particularly by the number two and number three players."

On home connectivity, MTN wants to aggressively play in the fibre market, but unlike Telkom, which spent years investing in fibre rollouts, it does not have extensive fibre assets.

"The build is completely off the table. South Africa has built enough fibre, and it would be a poor allocation of capital to say we're going to build fibre on our own," Mupita said.

"So on fibre-to-the-home, it's going to be partnering or it's going to be an acquisition at the right price at the right time."

data.title: Russia lists local apps that will survive its internet blackouts
data.description:
Russia published a list of locally developed social media, ride-hailing and other apps that it said would keep working during its mobile internet shutdowns - blackouts that have often been ordered to disrupt Ukrainian drone attacks.

The list issued on Friday included online government services, marketplaces, the Mir electronic payment system and state-backed messenger MAX. It omitted rival foreign services including Meta Platforms' WhatsApp.

The Digital Development Ministry said it had a "special technical solution" to let local apps keep going. "This measure will reduce the inconvenience caused to citizens by mobile internet shutdowns necessary to ensure security," it added.

It made no mention of Ukraine or drones. Governors from Russian border regions have regularly said blackouts were needed to disrupt assaults that use the internet to navigate to their targets.

Russia has also been increasingly keen to promote home-grown internet services and increase its control over the local online space.

It has restricted foreign apps, part of a broader clash between Moscow and foreign tech platforms that has intensified since the onset of the war in Ukraine in 2022.

Online monitoring services reported an increase in Russian internet users complaining about poor WhatsApp connectivity and periodic mobile outages this summer.

The ministry said it had compiled its list by identifying the "most popular and socially significant Russian services and websites".

Its focus on local apps left out Alphabet's YouTube and also WhatsApp, which was used by 97.6 million people in Russia in July, according to Mediascope data.

Second in those rankings, with 90.9 million users, was Telegram, a Dubai-based company founded by Russian-born Pavel Durov that was also not on the government list.

The third-placed VK Messenger, an offering from state-controlled tech company VK , reached 16.7 million people, according to the data.

MAX, which was also developed by VK and now comes pre-installed on all mobile phones and tablets sold in Russia, said this week it had 30 million users.
